1: The scene resets: the heavenly council (bene ha-’elohim) presents before YHWH again, and the adversary comes among them to present himself (lehityatsev ’al-YHWH). The verb yatsav in the hithpael = to take one’s stance, present oneself, stand (used of soldiers in formation and of judicial standing). The adversary is again in his position as the accusing officer of the divine court.
2: The adversary’s answer is verbatim from 1:7: missut ba-’arets umitthallek bah. The repetition is not careless; the prose prologue uses deliberate word-for-word repetition to highlight the structural parallelism of the two tests. The second scene is a controlled variation of the first: same setting, same question, different stakes.
3: YHWH’s response includes a significant new element: od hevel bemummatoh (he still maintains his integrity/wholeness). Tummah is the noun of the tam family, meaning completeness, moral wholeness, integrity. YHWH then acknowledges: watsetitehu bi (you incited/moved me against him) and lehabbel’o hinam (to ruin/swallow him for nothing). Balla’ = to swallow/destroy; hinam = for nothing (reprising 1:9 — the book’s thesis word). YHWH describes the adversary’s incitement and the destruction of Job as genuinely causeless (hinam), yet the test proceeded under YHWH’s permission.
4: ’Or be’ad ’or (skin in exchange for skin) — the adversary’s counter-claim is a commercial or proverbial formula whose exact meaning is debated. The most natural reading: one skin (one’s own life) will be given to protect another skin (possessions, children). The formula asserts that human self-interest is bottomless: when the real stakes (one’s own body) are raised, Job will crack. Kol ’asher le-’ish yitten be’ad nafsho (everything a man has he will give for his life) — the adversary’s anthropology: pure self-preservation at the core of every human.
5: ’ets ’atsamaw (his flesh and his bones) — the adversary adds the second condition: if YHWH strikes Job’s own body, he will curse God. The progression from 1:11 (strike what he has) to 2:5 (strike his body) tracks the escalation. ’Im-lo’ ’al-panekha yevarekheka — the same oath formula and curse-euphemism from 1:11, verbatim.
6: YHWH’s second permission: hinno veyadekha raq ’et-nafsho shomor (he is in your hands; only preserve his life). The limit is more specific now: the adversary may attack Job’s body but not take his life (nefesh). Nefesh = life-breath, person, the animating principle. The adversary has full access to Job’s physical existence but is bounded at the line of death.
7: Shehin ra’ (painful/festering sores) — shehin = an inflammatory skin disease; ra’ = severe/evil. The same word (shehin) is used for the Egyptian plague of boils (Exod 9:9–10) and the skin disease in Leviticus 13. Job is afflicted mikapo regel ’ad qoqodoh (from the sole of his foot to the top of his head) — the merism of totality: every point of his body. The comprehensiveness echoes the totality of the first round of disasters.
8: Job scrapes himself with a potsherd (heres) while sitting betok ha-’efer (in the midst of the ashes). The ashes are the standard location of mourning and lament (Ezek 27:30; Jer 6:26; Isa 58:5). The broken pottery as a scraper for diseased skin is a detail of physical degradation; Job moves from the position of greatest man of the east (1:3) to sitting among ashes scraping sores.
9: Job’s wife: ha-’odhekha mahaziq betummatekha (are you still holding fast in your integrity?) — hazaq = hold fast/be strong; tummah = the wholeness-integrity from vv3 and 1:1. Her advice: barekh ’elohim ve-mut (curse God and die). The same curse-euphemism (barakh) for third time in the prologue. The irony: Job’s wife uses the same euphemistic blessing-word that the adversary used. Whether the wife is acting as an agent of the adversary or as a broken woman in genuine despair is left ambiguous by the narrator.
10: Job’s rebuke: kedabber ’ahat hanevelot tedabberi (you are speaking as one of the foolish women speaks). Naval = senseless/foolish in a moral-practical sense — the person who acts as though there is no God, the same root as Nabal (1 Sam 25:25: “his name is Nabal and folly is with him”). Job’s response: ha-tov neqabbel me-’et ha-’elohim ve-’et hara’ lo neqabbel (shall we accept the good from God and not accept the adversity?). The verb qibbel = receive/accept; both good and adversity are received from the same source. The narrative verdict (v10b): lo-hata’ ’iyyov bifsatav — Job did not sin with his lips. Note the precision: not “in his heart” (as in 1:5’s concern about the children) but with his lips.
11: The three friends: ’Eliphaz ha-Temani (from Tema/Teman, in Edom, associated with wisdom, Jer 49:7), Bildad ha-Shuhi (from Shuah, possibly near the Euphrates), Tsofar ha-Naamati (from Naamah, uncertain location). All three are outsiders like Job; the wisdom dialogue takes place in a non-Israelite setting. Their purpose: lenud-lo (to express grief/sympathy to him) and lenakhamo (to comfort him) — two verbs of pastoral presence. Nud = to nod/wag the head in sympathy; naham = to comfort/console.
12: From a distance they could not recognize him (lo hikkiruhu) — Job’s transformation from the greatest man of the east is so complete that his own friends cannot identify him at a distance. The three gestures: wayyis’u qolam wayyivku (they lifted their voices and wept), wayyiqre’u ’ish me’ilo (each tore his outer garment), and wayyizrequ afar ’al-rosheihem hashamaymah (threw dust toward the sky over their heads). The dust thrown skyward is a grief gesture directed toward heaven — an appeal or expression of communal mourning before God.
13: Seven days and seven nights (shiv’at yamim veshiv’at leilot) of silent presence. Shiv’a (seven) is the number of the mourning week; cf. the seven-day mourning for Jacob (Gen 50:10). Velo-dibber ’elav dabar (and no one spoke a single word to him) — the friends’ silence before Job’s suffering is, paradoxically, the most theologically sound thing they do in the entire book. YHWH will later criticize their words (42:7) but the silence is not criticized. The narrator’s reason: ki ra’u ki gadal hake’ev me’od (they saw that the pain was very great). Ke’ev = anguish/pain.
